canonical_name: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
description: Palestinian Marxist-Leninist organization founded in 1967 by George Habash. It is the second-largest faction in the Palestine Liberation Organization, opposing Fatah's moderate stance and advocating a one-state solution. The group pioneered aircraft hijackings in the late 1960s and early 1970s and has been designated a terrorist organization by several countries. Its military wing is the Abu Ali Mustafa Brigades.

country_links.evidence_quote: The plane was diverted to Algiers, where 21 passengers and 11 crew members were held for 39 days, until 31 August.
country_links.context: The PFLP diverted the hijacked El Al Flight 426 to Algiers and held passengers there.

country_links.evidence_quote: on 9 September 1970, hijacked a BOAC flight from Bahrain to London via Beirut
country_links.context: The PFLP hijacked a BOAC flight from Bahrain.

country_links.evidence_quote: On 6 September 1970, the PFLP, including Leila Khaled, hijacked four passenger aircraft from Pan Am, TWA and Swissair on flights to New York from Brussels, Frankfurt and Zürich
country_links.context: The PFLP hijacked a Pan Am flight from Brussels.

country_links.evidence_quote: The Pan Am flight was diverted to Cairo
country_links.context: The PFLP diverted a hijacked Pan Am flight to Cairo.

country_links.evidence_quote: flights to New York from Brussels, Frankfurt and Zürich
country_links.context: The PFLP hijacked a Pan Am flight from Frankfurt.

country_links.evidence_quote: Gunmen opened fire on El Al Flight 253 in Athens about to take off for New York on 26 December 1968, killing one Israeli
country_links.context: PFLP gunmen opened fire on El Al Flight 253 in Athens.

country_links.evidence_quote: The hijacking of El Al Flight 426 from Rome to Lod airport in Israel on 23 July 1968.
country_links.context: The PFLP hijacked El Al Flight 426 from Rome, Italy.

country_links.evidence_quote: On 30 May 1972, 28 passengers were gunned down at Ben Gurion International Airport by members of the Japanese Red Army in collaboration with the PFLP's Waddie Haddad
country_links.context: The PFLP collaborated with the Japanese Red Army in the Lod Airport massacre.

country_links.evidence_quote: The TWA, Swissair and BOAC flights were diverted to Dawson's Field in Zarqa, Jordan. The TWA, Swissair and BOAC aircraft were subsequently blown up by the PFLP on 12 September
country_links.context: The PFLP diverted hijacked flights to Dawson's Field in Zarqa, Jordan, and blew them up there.

country_links.evidence_quote: hijacked a BOAC flight from Bahrain to London via Beirut
country_links.context: The PFLP hijacked a BOAC flight via Beirut.

country_links.evidence_quote: On 13 October 1977, the PFLP hijacked Lufthansa Flight 181, a Boeing 737 flying from Palma de Mallorca to Frankfurt.
country_links.context: The PFLP hijacked Lufthansa Flight 181 from Palma de Mallorca.

country_links.evidence_quote: An attack on El Al Flight 432 passengers jet at Zürich airport on 18 February 1969, killing the co-pilot and wounding the pilot
country_links.context: The PFLP attacked El Al Flight 432 passengers at Zürich airport.

country_links.evidence_quote: The hijacking of TWA Flight 840 from Los Angeles to Damascus on 29 August 1969 by a PFLP cell led by Leila Khaled
country_links.context: The PFLP hijacked TWA Flight 840 to Damascus.

country_links.evidence_quote: failed in an attempt to hijack an El Al aircraft which landed safely in London after one hijacker was killed and the other overpowered
country_links.context: The PFLP attempted to hijack an El Al aircraft which landed safely in London.

country_links.context: The PFLP hijacked TWA Flight 840 from Los Angeles.
